coredata.dc:title: Waste derived sustainable carbon nanodots as a new approach for sensitive quantification of ethionamide and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Gunjal, Datta B.
Gore, Anil H.
Bhosale, Amrut R.
Naik, Vaibhav M.
Anbhule, Prashant V.
Shejwal, Rajendra V.
Kolekar, Govind B.
coredata.dc:description: The quantification of Ethionamide (ETA) is of great importance due to their anti-tuberculosis activity and hazardous effect on humankind. Therefore, highly sensitive, selective simple and facile method for their detection will be highly appreciable. Herein we have attempted Carbon dot (C-CDs), synthesized from tender coconut waste, copiously available, by a simple hydrothermal treatment, as a fluorescent probe for the detection of ETA. The quenching of fluorescence of C-CDs with different concentrations of ETA is observed and this is due to the inner filter effect since absorption band of ETA overlaps with excitation and or emission of CDs. Under the optimized condition, a good linear quenching relationship is established in the range of 0–2.4 μg mL−1 with a limit of detection 0.33 μg mL−1. This proposed low-cost nanosensor system offers several advantages like simplicity, high selectivity, sensitivity, and rapid response. Hence, C-CDs based probe ultimately found excellent application for the detection of ETA in the urine and water samples giving a precise and highly reproducible result for these complex samples. Furthermore, C-CDs were anticipated for biological applications. The results revealed that C-CDs have less toxicity to the bacterial cells suggested excellent biocompatibility and ideal material for differentially staining of a yeast cell.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ethionamide
Inner filter effect
Nanosensor
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of water and urine samples
Detection of ETA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–vis and fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of synthesized C-CDs b) Excitation dependent emission spectra.
a) TEM image b) Particle size Distribution and c) XRD pattern of C-CDs.
a) FT-IR spectra, b) XPS wide scan, c) High resolution spectra of C1s and d) High resolution spectra of O1s of synthesized CDs.
a) Effect of pH b) Irradiation effect and c) Effect of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity of C-CDs.
a) Emission Spectra of C-CDs with different concentration of ETA b) Calibration curve.
a) Overlap spectra of C-CDs and ETA b) Life time of C-CDs with ETA c) Absorbance spectra of with different concentration of ETA.
Bar diagram of different interfering compounds in detection of ETA.
Fluorescence microscopy images of saccharomyces cerevisiae yeast cells after the incubation for 2 h with C-CDs (a,b,c,d) and without C-CDs (e,f,g,h).
Growth curve of S. cerevisiae in the presence of different volumes of C–CDS.
Schematic representation of synthesis of carbon dots and their applications.
Comparison of different methods for ETA determination.

row.entry: Facile, low cost, selective and biocompatible probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Real Sample analysis of ETA in different samples.

head.ce:title: Waste derived sustainable carbon nanodots as a new approach for sensitive quantification of ethionamide and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, 416 004, Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lal Bahadur Shastri College of Arts, Science and Commerce, Satara, 415002, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lal Bahadur Shastri College of Arts

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, 416 004, Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry Rajarshi Chhatrapati Sahu College, Kolhapur, 416005, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry Rajarshi Chhatrapati Sahu College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of carbon dots and their applications.

The quantification of Ethionamide (ETA) is of great importance due to their anti-tuberculosis activity and hazardous effect on humankind. Therefore, highly sensitive, selective simple and facile method for their detection will be highly appreciable. Herein we have attempted Carbon dot (C-CDs), synthesized from tender coconut waste, copiously available, by a simple hydrothermal treatment, as a fluorescent probe for the detection of ETA. The quenching of fluorescence of C-CDs with different concentrations of ETA is observed and this is due to the inner filter effect since absorption band of ETA overlaps with excitation and or emission of CDs. Under the optimized condition, a good linear quenching relationship is established in the range of 0–2.4 μg mL−1 with a limit of detection 0.33 μg mL−1. This proposed low-cost nanosensor system offers several advantages like simplicity, high selectivity, sensitivity, and rapid response. Hence, C-CDs based probe ultimately found excellent application for the detection of ETA in the urine and water samples giving a precise and highly reproducible result for these complex samples. Furthermore, C-CDs were anticipated for biological applications. The results revealed that C-CDs have less toxicity to the bacterial cells suggested excellent biocompatibility and ideal material for differentially staining of a yeast cell.

ce:section.ce:para: Tuberculosis (TB) is a widely known contagious disease, responsible for the death of millions of peoples in all over the world hence posed a great threat to the human kind from the years. The Mycobacterium tuberculosis, a disease causing microbes, is responsible for the infection of TB. Therefore, to control the disease the first-line anti-tuberculosis drugs like pyrazinamide, isoniazid, rifampin and ethambutol were employed in the 1960s. But, with the time the TB strain showed resistance to the first line drugs owing to their multidrug resistant (MDR) ability which once again became alarming. This eventually pays the way for the introduction of second-line drugs, have high recurrence rate, like kanamycin, amikacin, capreomycin, thiacetazone, ethionamide cycloserine and prothionamide for effective control of the TB [1,2].

Ethionamide, 2-ethylpyridine-4-carbothioamide, (ETA) is a bacteriostatic, bactericidal, anti-tuberculosis thioamide derivative. It is clinically effective and vital component due to their continuous use in the treatment of multidrug-resistant Mycobacterium tuberculosis as well as M. leprae and M. avium diseases [3]. Efficacies of ETA against bacilli lie in the activation of drug as well as combine use with other anti-tuberculosis drugs. The activating enzyme such as EthA, a mono-oxygenase in M. tuberculosis, is accountable for thioamide oxidation leading to the formation of sulfoxide metabolite which exerts toxic effect on the synthesis of mycolic acid through the inhibition of the activity of the inhA gene and ultimately affects the cell membrane of bacteria. An ETA is an orally administered drug, completely metabolized in the liver in both active i.e. ethionamide oxide and inactive metabolites and shows high effectiveness when applied in vivo rather than vitro. After the absorption, the almost 30% of drug is able to bind to the plasma protein while remaining becomes availability of drug. However, merely 5% of the administered ETA is found to be in the urine in unaltered form. Although ETA is promising drug against the M. tuberculosis, higher dose of it causes serious side effects. The main adverse effects are hepatotoxicity, endocrine effects, neurotoxicity, cardiovascular effects, hypothyroidism, skin reactions and gastrointestinal syndromes such as nausea, vomiting, metallic taste, anorexia, loss of appetite, abdominal pain, diarrhoea and weight loss [4]. Hence, in this context, it becomes essential to develop such a technique which can quantify ETA in various samples with a minimum precision.

So far many reports have been made to develop different analytical methods to monitor ETA in biological fluids as well as pharmaceutical formulations. Some of these are as chromatographic methods [5,6], capillary electrophoresis [7], fluorimetry [8], spectrophotometry [9], and electrochemical methods [10]. Bruno Regis LyrioFerraz et. al. has determined ethionamide (ETA) and pyrazinamide simultaneously using poly(L-cysteine) film-modified glassy carbon electrode by electrochemical method. However, these methods do not run away from drawbacks such as tedious process of operation, complication in assessment and sample preparation. Therefore, to develop reliable, simple, accurate, economical, reproducible and sensitive method for the quantification of ETA is an urgent need and eventually it comes to the spectrofluorometer which is highly sensitive, simple, accurate and non-destructive technique. Fluorescent nanomaterials like Cadmium sulphide quantum dots (CdS), Cadmium tellurium quantum dots (CdTe) and carbon dots (CD) have been synthesized and investigated by researcher since years as a biosensor, in optoelectronics, bioimaging, chemical sensors and so on. But among these CDs have endeavored as a novel fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of different cations, anions and biomolecules such as pesticide, amino acids, antibiotics etc. from the years. It is smart, shining, benign, inexpensive fluorescent member of carbaneous family with size less than 10 nm. Ease availability of precursor to synthesis CDs finds the innumerable applications in various fields like sensing, biological labelling, drug delivery, optoelectronics etc [11]. Although CDs exhibit lower quantum yield and broader emission spectra, it also have attractive properties like excellent biocompatibility less-toxicity, tunable luminescence, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, ease of surface modification, chemical inertness and stability which endow it as an ideal member for the said applications and used enormously compared to organic dyes and semiconducting quantum dyes. The different strategies like laser ablation, acidic oxidation, microwave or ultrasonic, hydrothermal, arc discharge and combustion or simple heating have been employed for the synthesis of CDs [11]. Even though aforementioned synthetic routes of CDs have some merits, they also have some disadvantages like the requirement of a high temperature, harsh synthetic conditions, a complex, multi-step operations, use of surface passivating agent to improve water solubility and strong acids and time-consuming process, hence eventually it ends with expensiveness of the methods, which confronts their wide spread applicability. Therefore, worthy concept is that synthesis of CDs from bio-precursors, a greener approach comes in mind. There are overwhelming reports on synthesis of CDs from ubiquitous waste carbon containing material [12]. Bibekan and De et al. reported synthesis of CDs from banana juice with high quantum yield by a simple heating while Adhimoorthy Prasannan et al. opted waste orange peel as a bio resource material for the synthesis of CDs [13,14]. There are different fluorescent chemosensors based on various photophysical properties and sensing mechanism have been developed. The most important fluorescence quenching phenomenon are fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT), metal–ligand charge transfer (MLCT), and intramolecular charge transfer(ICT), electronic energy transfer (EET) [15]. However these processes require interaction between probe and analyte and should be at specific distance which leads to the complicated and time consuming method. Therefore, alternative Inner filter effect (IFE) based sensors have enticed researches in recent days because it is simple, shows enhanced sensitivity and observed by absorption of light by analyte in the excitation and/or emission region of probe hence directly related with absorbance of analyte and fluorescence intensity [16,17].

Herein we attempted a tender coconut waste as a precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots. It is well known waste material and abundantly available biomass. Furthermore, these synthesized CDs were employed for the selective and sensitive detection of ETA based on inner filter effect. To the best of our knowledge, this is first report on CDs based fluorescent probe for determination of ETA. This is Facile, low cost, selective and sensitive, and biocompatible method compared to previously developed methods. The possible mechanism of interaction was based on the inner filter effect of ETA. Moreover, applicability of the developed method has been practiced in environmental and biological sample and cell imaging and toxicity.

Ethionamide was procured from TCI Chemicals (India) Pvt. Ltd. with 98% purity. Cysteine, EDTA, L-Tyrosine, N-acetyl L-cysteine (NAC), Ascorbic acid, Thiamine Hydrochloride and ATP were purchased from Hi-Media while metal salts used here are from S d fine-chem. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Citric acid, Boric acid and tri-sodium phosphate dodecahydrate used to prepare phosphate buffer solutions with pH range 2–12, were obtained from Molychem India. Double distilled water has been used to prepare stock solutions of the above compounds.

Photoluminescence spectra were recorded on Spectrofluorometer (JASCO Model FP–8300 Japan) with both excitation and emission slit was set at 5 nm band pass. A UV Specord Analytik jena UV–VIS spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorption spectra with 1.0 cm quartz cell at room temperature. Life time measurement has been performed on Time Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) Spectrophotometer HORIBA Jobin Yvon IBH with nanosecond diode laser (IBH, nano LED-07) as a source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by using a Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) on KBr plates. Powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with CuK α1 (λ = 1.5406 A) radiation with the voltage of 45 kV and current of 40 mA (Bruker, X-ray diffractometer) was used to determine the crystallinity of sample. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) used to determine the size of nanoparticles was obtained on a PHILIPS, CM-200 model instrument with an accelerating voltage 20-200kv. The chemical composition was defined by Kratos AXIS ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Confocal microscopic analysis was carried out on Zeiss Axio-scope A 1 trinocular phase contrast fluorescent microscope at different excitation wave length.

The waste material of tender Coconut was cut into small pieces followed by carbonization process. To obtain fine powder, it was further ground and sieved. This whole powder was then subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 4 h with some water and eventually, we got the black residue. Furthermore, it was dispersed in distilled water and filtered through Whatman filter paper. The obtained yellowish carbon dots solution was further centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove coarse particles of carbon dots. Moreover, it was again filtered through 0.45μM PVDF membrane filter and then subjected to dialysis (MWCO-3000d) against double distilled water for 24 h. This dialyzed solution was diluted up to certain volume and kept at freeze temperature for further experiment (Scheme 1 ).

The typical procedure recommended for the detection of ETA is as follow: into the 5 mL standard volumetric flask, 1.0 mL of as prepared carbon dots (C-CDs) and aliquot of ETA with different concentration were added separately. Then, each mixture was thoroughly mixed and diluted up to the mark with double distilled water. Furthermore, all the above solutions were allowed to react at room temperature for 10 min and followed by fluorescent measurement at 320 nm with slit width 5 nm for both excitation and emission. Moreover, calibration curve obtained from fluorescence measurement has been used for the quantitative analysis of ETA.

Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain was grown in Nutrient agar broth (Hi-media, India) overnight at 28 °C. For staining, cell culture was washed and re-suspended in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4). Furthermore 20 μL of C-CDs was mixed with cell suspension and incubated 28 °C for 2 h. After that, the cell suspension (1 mL) was aseptically withdrawn and mounted on a microscopic glass slide. Fluorescent microscopic analysis was carried out on Zeiss Axio-scope A 1 trinocular phase contrast microscope with laser excitation at 405, 488 and 561 nm. The fluorescence was observed in blue, green and red regions. The toxicity study of C-CDs was performed against S. cerevisiae in 150 mL Erlenmeyer flasks at 37 °C for 24 h. Briefly, to these flasks, cell culture were seeded in nutrient agar medium followed by varying amount of CDs (0, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μL) were added and incubated by using orbital shaker. At regular intervals, sample was collected and their optical density was measured at 620 nm to know cell viability using a spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of water and urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of ETA was carried out in human urine and tap water samples. The urine sample from a healthy person was collected and kept at freeze temperature overnight to settle impurities of the sample. 1 mL of the above solution was further diluted to 10 mL and successively used for the analysis by standard addition method. Tap water was collected from our laboratory and filtered through Whatman filter paper to remove suspended particles. Thus, obtained filtrate was further subjected to the analysis. Both urine and water samples were spiked with three different concentration of ETA and subjected to the developed sensing system followed by fluorescence measurement.

The carbon dots synthesized from tender coconut waste material (C-CDs) by carbonization followed by hydrothermal treatment was yellow coloured and transparent solution which represents excellent aqueous solubility. The optical properties of the C-CDs were characterized by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. The UV absorption spectra do not show any characteristic peak which is in accordance with the previous report. (Fig. 1 a) [18,19]. The maximum emission intensity of C-CDs observed at 430 nm when excited at 320 nm (Fig. 1a). This result reflects that C-CDs have an excellent luminescence property in water. So detailed photoluminescence property were elaborated with different excitation wavelength. The fluorescence emission spectra of C-CDs are varied with the excitation wavelength from 300 to 360 nm with 10 nm increment and presented in Fig. 1b. There was change in emission intensity observed at each wavelength while peak position showed red shift with increment which is shown in normalized inset of Fig. 1b. These results reveal that well known excitation dependent emission phenomenon of carbon dots which is similar with previous report [20].

Particle size and morphology of the C-CDs was assessed by the TEM. The TEM images clearly indicate C-CDs are spherically shaped, smaller in size and well dispersed in the solution (Fig. 2 a). The average size distribution of prepared C-CDs was found to be 2.5 nm represented by the histogram in Fig. 2b.The XRD pattern of the C-CDs in Fig. 2c shows a broad diffraction peak suggest that amorphous nature of CDs which is in accordance with previous report [21].

FT-IR spectroscopy is also used for the identification of functional groups associated to the surface of C-CDs. The broad absorption at 3341 cm−1is due to the vibrational stretching frequency of OH bond. The peak at 2934 cm−1is ascribed to the methyl or CH vibration. The characteristic absorption at 1713 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching. Moreover, CC has vibrational stretching band at 1633 cm−1. While peat at 1219 cm−1 and 963 cm−1 are assigned to the COC and CO vibrations. All these absorption peaks evident that the CDs synthesized from tender coconut waste are composed of different functional groups likeOH, COOH. These functional groups endowed excellent water solubility for C-CDs. (Fig. 3 a) In addition to this, chemical composition and chemical bonding in synthesized C-CDs were evaluated by XPS measurement. The XPS wide scan spectrum of the C-CDs (Fig. 3b)) displayed two prominent peaks at 285.56 and 531.12 eV, indicated synthesized C-CDs mainly composed of carbon (72.08%) and oxygen (27.92%) [22]. Fig. 3c shows deconvolution of the C1s spectrum, which indicated the three main peaks at 284.7,287.3 and 288.7 eV, attributed to the CC, CO and CO bonds respectively. Moreover, the high resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 3d) display two peaks at 530.4 eV and 532.6 eV, which can be due to the CO and COH/COC groups respectively [23]. These binding energy peaks suggested that both high resolution spectrum is consistent with each other. These results also revealed that C-CDs surface mainly passivated by multiple carbonyl and hydroxyl groups which were similar with above mentioned FT-IR spectroscopy.

Stability of Carbon dots is a very important aspect as far as their practical application concern. Herein we have considered pH, ionic strength of solution and irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. To check the consequence of pH on the emission intensity of C-CDs, the pH solutions within the range of 2–12 was prepared by mixing the appropriate amount of Boric acid, Citric acid, and trisodium phosphate. Furthermore, the C-CDs were mixed with desired pH solutions and then emission spectra were recorded. As Fig. 4 a, reveals that there is slight variation in fluorescence intensity with pH but it is negligible from a practical point of view. Hence it could be concluded that as synthesized C-CDs have an almost pH independent emission intensity. The emission intensity of C-CDs also did not show variation with continuous irradiation of sample under a Xenon lamp with excitation wavelength at 320 nm with 10 min time interval between two readings (Fig. 4b). In addition, the effect of ionic strength, is one of the key factor on luminescence of C-CDs was also investigated in different concentration of NaCl solutions (0.1 to 1.0 M). Fig. 4c displays no obvious change in photoluminescence behavior of C-CDs in salt solution. All these observations like marvelous photostability, capable to resist highly ionic strength solution, excellent pH tolerance and photobleaching makes them a promising candidate for a wide range of potential applications and commercial purpose.

The astonishing optical properties of the C-CDs encouraged us to employ it as a probe for various potential applications. Fig. 5 a displays the gradual dwindling of fluorescence intensity of C-CDs with diverse concentrations of ETA without any significant shift in the spectra. The aforementioned steady state fluorescence quenching was exhibited response with ETA within 0–2.4 μg mL−1 range. Further, this fluorescence quenching efficiency was interpreted by well known Stern-Volmer plot (Eq. 1).
(1) F0/F = 1+ KSV [Q] = 1+ k τ0 [Q]
Where F0 and F are the emission intensities in absence and presence of analyte respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of an analyte. So, by using this the Stern-Volmer equation, plot of F0/F versus concentration of ETA was plotted. Fig. 5b displays linear response of C-CDs with the whole concentration range of ETA (0–2.4 μg mL−1). The linear proportionality was illustrated by regression equation F0/F = 0.564x + 1 with corresponding correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998, where x is the concentration of ETA. The limit of detection (LOD) of the established method was calculated by the following equation (Eq. 2)
(2) Limit of Detection (LOD) = 3.3σ/K
Where σ is the relative standard deviation of the corrected blank C-CDs sample and K is the slope of the calibration curve. Thus, LOD of the proposed method was found to be 0.33 μg mL−1. The assessment of LOD and linear range of different methods for ETA determination is summarized in Table 1 . From this observation, our developed sensing method predicted the comparable limit of detection LOD and very wide linear range than previously reported analytical techniques. Along with this, high selectivity, comparable sensitivity, reproducibility and first time report make it a novel and important approach for the detection of ETA. In addition to this, the proposed method is low cost, simple, green and novel for ETA determination. Thus, these observations eventually lead to their use for quantitative determination of ETA in tap water as well as in biomedical (Urine) sample. So, this work is expected to open new opportunities for the development of the C-CDs based fluorescence assay.

The dwindling of fluorescence of C-CDs was observed with increasing concentration of ETA. This process might be attributed to the various quenching mechanisms such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) etc. The insight of such mechanism could be gained from optical properties of probe and analyte. Fig. 6 a displays a spectral overlap between the absorption band of the ETA and the excitation as well as emission spectrum of the C-CDs which accounts either FRET or IFE quenching mechanism. Meanwhile, the lifetime of C-CDs in presences of 1.0 and 2.0 μg mL−1 concentration of ETA were recorded which is effective path to discriminate mode of quenching since in the IFE process two species do not chemically interact with each other therefore there would not be any change in the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore. As shown in Fig. 6b, fluorescence decay profile yield lifetimes 2.1, 2.08 and 2.01 ns with different Concentration of ETA. The average life time of C-CDs remained unchanged, rules out the possibility of FRET between C-CDs and ETA. Thus, IFE is the steering cause of the decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs. In this process, excitation as well as emission light of CDs is also shielded by the absorbance of ETA which eventually leads to the fluorescence quenching of C-CDs ensures that the IFE occurs in a highly efficient way. Moreover, absorption spectra do not show any new absorption peak after addition of ETA, revealed that rare possibility of non-fluorescent ground-state complex formation between them. (Fig. 6c)

Performance of the sensor is based on the outstanding selectivity to the analyte of interest. Therefore, the selectivity of this proposed system was evaluated by the monitoring response of the different coexisting ions, molecules with an equal concentration of analyte (ETA) on fluorescence intensity of C-CDs. From the Fig. 7 , it is evident that the FL intensity of C-CDs exhibits a noticeable quenching to the ETA while other ions and molecules such as Na+, K+, SO4 2– Cl–, L-Tyrosine, N-acetyl L-cysteine, Ascorbic acid, Thiamine hydrochloride, Cysteine, ATP and EDTA have merely or little effects on intensity. This observation demonstrates excellent specificity of the C-CDs towards the ETA which might be due to the inner filter effect. Thus, good sensitivity as well as selectivity of the proposed method makes them suitable for real time applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ETA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed nanosensor was explored in real samples such as human urine and tap water. Standard addition method was employed for the detection of ETA in both the samples. Furthermore, the accuracy and reliability of the detection method were evaluated through a recovery study. As listed in Table 2 , the obtained recoveries of samples varied from 97.7% to 101.6%, recoveries were well above 95% in all cases, suggested acceptable average recoveries in both the samples which indicated good stability and anti-interference ability of the developed method. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of each sample was also around 3%. reveals better precision and excellent reproducibility. The proposed method has good recoveries, low RSD, high reproducibility, sensitivity and selectivity indicating potential applications in the detection of ETA in human Urine and tap water.

In summary, we have synthesized C-CDs by simple carbonization followed by hydrothermal process from tender coconut waste. The as synthesized C-CDs was further characterized with various technique, the results obtained were consistent with previous reports. Furthermore, we have constructed C-CDs as a fluorescent probe for the quantification of ETA owing to their fluorescence quenching phenomenon. The inner filter effect was the main cause of such quenching. Moreover, the applicability this developed nanosensor were tested for the urine and water samples. The observation showed good sensitivity, selectivity and excellent recoveries which explores this proposed method for the quantification of ETA in real sample also. The developed method is simple, green and low cost compared to other techniques. Furthermore, this synthesized fluorescent probe showed excellent cell permeability low cytotoxicity and thus used for yeast cell imaging.
No any conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the University Grants Commission New Delhi India for providing financial support to this work under the scheme of NET-JRF. (F.17-11/2008(SA-I)).
